headline: Trump says US negotiating with Iran's parliamentary speaker Mohammad Bagher Qaliba, who denies talks
content:
US President Donald Trump on Monday threatened widespread destruction of Iran's energy resources and other vital infrastructure, including desalination plants, if a deal to end the war with Tehran is not reached "shortly."
Trump said the US is negotiating with Iran's parliamentary speaker, Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf, in an interview with the New York Post published Monday.
Read more: US reportedly strikes Iran’s Isfahan with 2,000-pound bunker-buster bombs; Trump shares video
The former Revolutionary Guard commander was previously floated as Washington's negotiating partner, but has denied Iran is talking to the US and said Pakistan-facilitated discussions were merely a cover for American troop deployments.
Read more: High-risk US plan to extract Iran’s uranium under review by Trump; Netanyahu claims war turning point
Meanwhile, Israel has invaded southern Lebanon to push out Iran-backed Hezbollah militants, who have fired rockets and drones across the border, in a campaign that Israeli officials suggest could become a prolonged occupation. Three UN peacekeepers were killed in southern Lebanon in less than 24 hours, but it's unclear who was responsible.
US stocks edged higher in shaky trading Monday as oil prices keep climbing because of uncertainty about when the war could end.
Drone hits a Kuwaiti oil tanker in Dubai waters, causing a fire, Dubai Media Office says
Authorities are working to control the fire, the Dubai Media Office said. All 24 crew members are safe, and no injuries have been reported, the office said.
The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations centre, run by the British military, reported the strike, saying the vessel was 31 nautical miles (57 kilometers) northwest of Dubai.
It said an unknown projectile struck their tanker on the starboard side, causing a fire on the vessel. No environmental impact has been reported, the centre said.
Israel says it is reviewing reports of UN peacekeepers killed in Lebanon
The Israeli army said early Tuesday it is aware of reports regarding two instances in which United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon peacekeepers were killed.
The army said and is reviewing them to determine whether the deaths resulted from Hezbollah activity or Israeli fire, noting that "these incidents occurred in an active combat area."
The statement comes after UNIFIL said three peacekeepers have been killed in southern Lebanon within 24 hours in two separate instances.
An explosion of "unknown origin" on Monday destroyed a vehicle near the village of Bani Hayyan, killing two peacekeepers and wounding two others, including one with severe injuries. Earlier, an Indonesian peacekeeper was killed after a base was hit by a projectile.
Saudi Arabia's defence ministry says it downed 3 ballistic missiles
The ministry said the missiles were launched toward its capital, Riyadh.
4 wounded after interception debris causes fire in Dubai
Emergency teams responded early Tuesday to a fire in an abandoned house in Al Badaa, a residential area in Dubai, after debris fell following an air defense interception, injuring four people, the Dubai Media Office said.
In Bahrain, authorities said warning sirens were sounded.
Gulf allies privately make the case to Trump to keep fighting until Iran is decisively defeated
Gulf allies of the United States are arguing that Tehran hasn't been weakened enough by the monthlong US-led bombing campaign, according to US, Gulf and Israeli officials.
Officials from Saudi Arabia, United Arab Emirates, Kuwait and Bahrain have conveyed in private conversations that they do not want the military operation to end until there are significant changes in the Iranian leadership or there's a dramatic shift in Iranian behaviour, according to the officials, who were not authorized to comment publicly and spoke on the condition of anonymity.
While regional leaders are broadly supportive now of the US efforts, one Gulf diplomat described some division, with Saudi Arabia and the UAE leading the calls for increasing military pressure on Tehran.
The UAE has emerged as perhaps the most hawkish of the Gulf countries and is pushing hard for Trump to order a ground invasion, the diplomat said.

headline: U.S.-Israel-Iran war puts India’s EV gap in focus
content:
The surge in global oil prices after the U.S.-Iran war has exposed the differing levels of energy vulnerability in Asia’s two largest economies, India and China. Since the conflict began on February 28, crude and refined fuel prices have increased rapidly, with the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) basket price rising by about 67% between February 27 and March 27, pushing up petrol, diesel and LPG costs.
The rise in fuel costs has revived interest in alternatives such as Electric Vehicles (EVs), plug-in hybrids and electric two-wheelers, especially in countries that rely heavily on imported oil.
The war has also increased the risk of supply disruptions due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which about one-fifth of global oil supply normally passes. China received 5.4 million barrels of crude oil per day via the Strait in FY25Q1, the highest in volume. India followed at 2.1 million barrels per day. But while both countries depend on imported oil, China’s faster adoption of EVs has reduced its transport sector’s exposure to fuel shocks, whereas India’s transport sector remains heavily reliant on fossil fuels.
Data on EV penetration show the scale of the gap between the two countries. In March 2026 alone, new-energy vehicles accounted for about 52.9% of passenger car sales in China, according to estimates by the China Passenger Car Association, while in India, EVs made up only about 6% of new car registrations in 2026.
Absolute sales numbers highlight the contrast. China sold about nine lakh new-energy passenger vehicles in March 2026 alone, whereas India registered about 72,000 electric cars in the last three months. In the two- and three-wheeler segment, China sold more than 72 lakh electric vehicles in 2024, while India’s sales even in 2026 were only about 4.27 lakh.
Because China began electrification earlier, the total number of EVs in use is far larger. China’s electric car fleet had reached about 2.3 crore by 2024, compared with about 3.96 lakh in India in 2026. China had about 6.8 crore electric two- and three-wheelers in use, while India has about 23 lakh. Overall, India’s total EV stock stood at about 27.3 lakh, still far below China’s levels.
Infrastructure and supply chains also reflect the gap. As of February 2026, India has about 14 electric cars per public charger, compared with roughly nine in China by the end of 2025, indicating higher charging availability which has allowed China to push EV adoption faster in the passenger vehicle segment.
These differences have direct implications during oil crises. Countries with high EV adoption are less exposed to sudden increases in petrol and diesel prices because a larger share of transport runs on electricity.
The recent increases in fuel price across several Asian countries following the Iran conflict have strengthened the argument for accelerating the transition to electric mobility. The shift is already visible in the market. Bloomberg recently reported that demand for EVs has increased across Asia after the Iran oil shock, with Chinese carmakers such as BYD seeing higher showroom traffic as consumers shift away from fossil fuel-driven vehicles.
India has made progress, especially in electric two-wheelers, but adoption in cars remains low and charging infrastructure limited, compared to China. As long as petrol, diesel and LPG remain the backbone of transport, every geopolitical conflict in West Asia will continue to be felt directly in Indian households.
Published - March 31, 2026 07:00 am IST

headline: Democratic lawmaker says US ‘badly and embarrassingly losing’ war with Iran
content:
US senator Chris Murphy, a Democrat from Connecticut, said the Donald Trump administration has “no strategy” against Iran and is “badly and embarrassingly losing the war”.
Murphy, a prominent member of the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, is among a growing group of lawmakers urging an immediate halt to the ongoing joint US–Israel military operation in Iran, now in its second month.
In a series of posts on X, Murphy wrote, “Tactics don’t matter in the absence of strategy. That’s why the ‘tactic’ of killing thousands of North Vietnamese or Taliban did nothing but help our enemy. The tactics didn’t serve a strategy.”
“Same thing in Iran. Our tactics are failing and that’s why Iran is succeeding.”
Here's the problem. These are all "tactical" objectives. They are not strategy. Because there IS NO strategy. 1/ Let me take these "objectives" 1-4 to explain - based upon what I've learned in briefings - why we are badly and embarrassingly losing this war. https://t.co/WULxwlEx0N— Chris Murphy 🟧 (@ChrisMurphyCT) March 30, 2026
His response came as he shared US Secretary of State Marco Rubio’s remarks on four key objectives of the US military in Iran.
“This operation, okay, and that’s what this is, it’s about very specific objectives. The president laid them out on the first night of the operation. I’ll repeat them to you now because I hear a lot of talk about, we don’t know what the clear objectives are,” said Rubio, pointing to the goals one-by-one.
Number one, he said, was the destruction of their air force. Second, the destruction of their navy. Third, the severe diminishing of their missile launching capability. And number four, the destruction of their factories so they “can’t make more missiles and more drones to threaten us in the future”.
“All of this so that they can never hide behind it to acquire a nuclear weapon. That was our objective from the beginning; that remains our objective now. We are on pace and in fact ahead of schedule on some of those things, and we are going to achieve those things in a number of weeks, not in a number of months,” Rubio stated.
Murphy responded to the objectives one-by-one and warned that the war would only create “a tighter China-Iran alliance”.

headline: Iran-Israel war LIVE updates: Kuwait says Iranian attack sparks fire on fully loaded tanker at Dubai port; issues oil spill warning
content:
U.S. President Donald Trump said on Tuesday (March 31, 2026) “the hard part” in the war against Iran was done and asked other countries affected by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz to buy oil from America or go to the Strait and take it. Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth said on Tuesday that talks on ending the war on Iran are making progress even as the month-long U.S.-Israeli military campaign against Iran continued.
U.S.-Israeli airstrikes on Tuesday hit one of the largest pharmaceutical companies in Iran which produces anaesthetic and cancer drugs, the Iranian government said. The Israeli military said on Tuesday four soldiers had been killed in combat in southern Lebanon where its forces are clashing in Iran-backed Hezbollah. The deaths bring the total number of troops killed in the war to 10.
Also read: Trump threatens to strike Iran’s civilian infrastructure if no deal is reached
Authorities said the blaze on a fully loaded Kuwaiti oil tanker at a Dubai port was extinguished. An assessment of the damage was under way amid warning of a possible oil spill in surrounding waters. Amir Sultan Air Base in eastern Saudi Arabia, hosting U.S. forces, came under drone attacks on Tuesday (March 31, 2026), Iranian Press TV said.
Also Read: U.S.-Israel-Iran war puts India’s EV gap in focus
Iran has denied reports of negotiations so far and said it has got a proposal from Washington which is not acceptable. It has made its own counter-proposal.

headline: Trump again threatens widespread destruction in Iran if a deal is not reached 'shortly'
content:
Trump again threatens widespread destruction in Iran if a deal is not reached 'shortly'
DUBAI, United Arab Emirates (AP) — U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday threatened widespread destruction of Iran’s energy resources and other vital infrastructure, potentially including desalination plants that supply drinking water, if a deal to end the war is not reached “shortly.”
Iran, meanwhile, struck a key water and electrical plant in Kuwait, and an oil refinery in Israel came under attack. A drone hit a Kuwaiti oil tanker in Dubai waters, causing a fire that authorities were working to control early Tuesday, the Dubai Media Office said.

headline: US-Iran War LIVE: Tanker struck at Dubai Port by Tehran, oil spill warning issued
content:
The war between US-Israel and Iran has crossed the one-month mark now, leaving thousands dead and disrupting global energy markets. Yet, the war appears to be far from over, with the Houthis joining in over the weekend and no concrete developments in talks. ‘Take the oil’ or talk it out? US President Donald Trump, over the weekend, appeared to be in two minds. On one hand, the Trump said Washington was looking to "take the oil" from Iran, and was even considering taking Iran's crude export hub, Kharg Island. On the other, he claimed that Tehran had accepted "most of" his 15-point ceasefire plan. However, Iran has publicly denied being in negotiations with the US, though reports suggest that communication is taking place via intermediaries and back channels. Reports also indicate that the Trump administration is mulling deploying an additional 10,000 troops in the region, including at least 5,000 US Marines and thousands of paratroopers from the famed 82nd Airborne. CENTCOM over the weekend said that 3,500 troops and sailors had reached the region aboard the USS Tripoli. Oil prices soar Since the war began, the Brent crude oil benchmark has risen by more than 50%, and the steady rise in oil prices has not slowed. Amid the effective closure of the crucial Strait of Hormuz, oil prices have continued to climb, with Brent crude touching $116 on Monday and the WTI benchmark settling at $102.88, its highest closing level since July 2022. Although Iran has opened the strait to what it deems as friendly nations — including India, Pakistan, Russia, and China — a majority of tankers remain anchored near the strategic waterway amid Iran's threats to non-friendly vessels. Regional disruptions continue With Iran retaliating against US and Israeli strikes, the entire Middle East has been plunged into chaos, with Gulf countries routinely reporting interceptions of missiles and drones launched by Tehran. On Monday, an Iranian attack on the giant Al-Salmi oil tanker at the Dubai Port caused a fire, with the Kuwait Petroleum Corporation warning that it could lead to an oil spill. US Secretary of Defence, Pete Hegseth, says, “I had the opportunity to visit our troops fighting in Operation Epic Fury... I watched a private first class confidently calling out an enemy missile trajectory to a room full of officers. Everyone focused; the room was locked in. Two enemy missiles were successfully shot down...” “I met a junior airman as the sun was going down and a chill was setting on the tarmac who, when asked what she needed, simply looked up at me with a smile on her face and said, more bombs, and bigger bombs. We will happily oblige her...,” he said. “I witnessed urgency to finish the job, urgency to achieve mission success, not looking at the next rotation, only moving as fast as possible to win... A crew chief we flew with summed it up nicely. He said, it's been a busy few weeks, sir, tough stuff, but I'm so honoured to be called up. This fight is long overdue. We need to address it for our kids. We cannot pass the buck. Please thank the President from us,” Pete Hegseth said. Israel will destroy all homes in Lebanese villages near the border and 600,000 people who fled the south will not be allowed home until northern Israel is secure, the defence minister said on Tuesday, vowing to inflict Gaza-like destruction in the area. Israel Katz reiterated Israeli plans to establish a buffer zone in southern Lebanon, saying that it would maintain control over a swathe of territory up to the Litani River once the war with Iran-backed Hezbollah group ended. Iran's foreign minister, in a call with his Turkish counterpart, warned of repeated false-flag operations aimed at undermining regional ties, the foreign ministry said, a day after NATO intercepted a ballistic missile fired from Iran over Turkey. Turkey is playing a mediating role, alongside other regional countries, between Washington and Tehran to help de-escalate tensions and end the war. Amid strikes in Dubai, Qatar has said Gulf states 'unified' in calling for war de-escalation. Brussels has reportedly urged EU states to 'coordinate measures' in face of oil price hike Three Chinese ships recently sailed through the Strait of Hormuz after coordination with relevant parties, a foreign ministry spokesperson told a regular daily press briefing on Tuesday, while calling for peace and stability in the Gulf Region. The critical waterway has effectively been shut since the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran began on February 28. Strikes damage desalination plant on Iran island in Strait of Hormuz: media, the Associated Press quoted media as saying. An explosion was heard Tuesday morning near the international airport of Erbil, a city in Iraq's autonomous Kurdistan region, news agency AFP reported citing one of its journalists. Erbil is home to a major US consulate complex, while its airport houses military advisers attached to a US-led international anti-jihadist coalition, the news agency added. CENCOM Commander Admiral Brad Cooper “visited with U.S. troops deployed to multiple locations in support of Operation Epic Fury. He recognized more than 40 service members with medals for their exceptional performance during the first weeks of the operation,” a release by CENTCOM said. The Hosseinieh Azam Zanjan Mosque, a renowned Shi'ite mosque and religious complex in Zanjan, was damaged by US-Israeli strikes, Iran's Fars news agency reported. The strikes damaged parts of the dome and minarets, and further inspections are being carried out to assess the damage. Explosions were heard in Dubai on Tuesday, news agency AFP reported citing journalists on the ground. Further details are awaited. Earlier in the day, Dubai authorities successfully doused a fire sparked by Iranian strikes on a Kuwaiti tanker anchored at the Dubai Port. Two container ships belonging to the Chinese shipping giant Cosco successfully passed through the Strait of Hormuz, exiting the Gulf, news agency AFP reported citing ship tracking data. The CSCL Indian Ocean crossed the strait at around 0914 GMT on Monday, followed by CSCL Arctic Ocean 27 minutes later, according to data from Marine Traffic. An attack on the city of Mahallat in Iran's Markazi province last night killed 11 people and injured 15 others, Al Jazeera reported on Tuesday citing Iran's ISNA news agency. Three residential units in Mahallat city were “directly hit by projectiles”, causing significant damage, the report said. The Israeli Defense Forces (IDF) on Tuesday said that it had eliminated dozens of 'terrorists' in southern Lebanon in the past 24 hours. “The forces destroyed warehouses where numerous weapons caches, headquarters, underground shafts and a launcher were located towards the territory of the country. Among the weapons located were military vests, helmets, and loaded RPG weapons,” the IDF statement added. The IDF also said that artillery operating in the area have fired more than 700 shells in recent days at targets Hezbollah targets in southern Lebanon. There was no oil spill from an Iranian strike on a Kuwaiti tanker at the Dubai Port, authorities said on Tuesday, after the Kuwait Petroleum had earlier warned of a possible spillage from the attack. Earlier in the day, the Dubai Media Office had announced that the fire on board the tanker had been successfully extinguished. Massive 2,000-pound bunker buster bombs were used to strike a large ammunition depot in the Iranian city of Isfahan, The Wall Street Journal reported on Tuesday citing a US official, adding that a "high volume" of such munitions were deployed in the attack. The comment by the official came after President Donald Trump posted an uncaptioned video on Truth Social showing a massive explosion followed by a chain of explosions. The video captured the strike, the official told WSJ. Thousands of Iranians gathered in Karaj to show support to the government and the armed forces amid the war in the Middle East, a clip shared by Iran's Press TV showed. The Israeli Defense Forces (IDF) on Tuesday announced the deaths of four Israeli soldiers in operations in southern Lebanon. The IDF also said that three soldiers had been hurt. Explosions were heard in Tehran and power cuts hit some parts of the capital city on Tuesday, Iranian media reports said. “Power outages in parts of Tehran after multiple explosions heard,” said the Fars news agency reported, while Tasnim reported hat some residents in the eastern part of the city were without electrcity. Israel's military on Tuesday said its air defences were responding to Iranian missiles. "A short while ago, the IDF identified missiles launched from Iran toward the territory of the State of Israel," the Israeli Defense Forces (IDF) posted on Telegram. Meanwhile, news agency AFP reported that at least 10 blasts were heard over Jerusalem. Iran's Press TV on Tuesday shared footage reporting that Israeli air defences had failed to intercept an Iranian missile, with blasts reported across Palestinian territories. US President Donald Trump could end the ongoing military campaign against Iran even if the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed. According to a report by The Wall Street Journal, which cited Trump administration officials, Washington is recalibrating its war objectives, and will focus instead on crippling Iran's navy, degrading its missile stockpile, and reducing its ability to threaten shipping through the crucial waterway. Air India and its low-cost subsidiary Air India Express have confirmed they will together operate 36 scheduled and non-scheduled flights to and from the West Asia region on 31 March 2026, amid the ongoing war. Check the routes here. The US Central Command (CENTCOM) on Monday (local time) released fresh footage of American strikes against Iran. It did not specify what the targets shown were. Authorities in Dubai extinguished a fire that had broken out after Iranian strikes on a Kuwaiti tanker at the Dubai port. “Relevant teams continue to assess the situation and take the necessary measures, and updates will be shared as they become available,” the Dubai Media Office added. Iran's Press TV early on Tuesday claimed citing reports that the Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia had been attacked by drones again. It did not provide any more details. Earlier, Iran had struck the base in Al Kharj and had shared photos claiming significant damage to an American E-3 Sentry AWACS aircraft. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi on Tuesday declared Saudi Arabia a “brotherly nation”, saying it deeply respected the kingdom. Sharing photos of the destruction caused by Iran's attack on the Prince Sultan Air Base, Araghchi explained that Tehran's strikes were aimed at US and Israeli forces, and also urged Saudi Arabia to oust American forces. “High time to eject US forces,” wrote Araghchi. US Secretary of War Pete Hegseth and Joint Chiefs of Staff chair Dan Caine are slated to hold a press conference at 8 am EST (6.30 pm IST) on Tuesday. US President Donald Trump on Monday local time shared a video showing a massive explosion. Trump did not provide any description or context. Watch the video below. Spain on Monday closed its airspace to US planes involved in attacks on Iran, a step beyond its previous denial of use of jointly-operated military bases, Spanish newspaper El Pais reported, citing military sources. The closure of Spain's airspace, which forces military planes to bypass the country to their targets in the Middle East, does not include emergency situations, El Pais added. "Two UNIFIL peacekeepers were tragically killed in south Lebanon today, when an explosion of unknown origin destroyed their vehicle near Bani Hayyan," the UN force in Lebanon, UNIFIL, said. “A third peacekeeper was severely injured, and a fourth was also hurt,” it added. A broker acting on behalf of Pete Hegseth, the US Defence Secretary, reportedly attempted to secure a multimillion-dollar investment in a major defence-focused fund in the weeks before Washington launched military strikes against Iran, a disclosure that the Pentagon has moved swiftly to deny. Authorities in Dubai were working to contain the fire on a Kuwaiti oil tanker struck by Iran at the city's port, the Dubai Media Office said. It did not clarify whether the fire had been contained. Iran on Monday attacked a fully-loaded crude oil tanker at Dubai Port's anchorage, Kuwait's state news agency KUNA said, setting it ablaze and damaging its hull. The Kuwait Petroleum Corp, meanwhile, warned of a possible oil spill. The apparent strike by Tehran is just the latest in a string of assaults on merchant vessels by missiles or explosive air and sea drones in the Gulf and Strait of Hormuz since the US and Israel attacked Iran on February 28. Shiladitya Ray specializes in covering geopolitics and science, and believes in communicating complex information through accessible, compelling, and if possible, visually engaging narratives. He has nearly 10 years of experience in digital media, and has been an Associate Editor with Mint for five months.

Shiladitya holds a bachelor's degree in English Literature from Jadavpur University, and two master's degrees in Development Studies and Sociology from TISS, Hyderabad and Delhi School of Economics respectively.

Shiladitya has also completed a Data Journalism fellowship with Google News Initiative (GNI), where he was a standout performer. He was subsequently invited as a speaker to GNI's AI Skills Workshop held in 2025, where he shared his previous work and experience in leveraging generative AI tools for data visualization with an audience of senior newsroom editors.

Prior to joining Mint, Shiladitya was a Chief Sub-Editor with Deccan Herald, and has previously worked for digital media startups NewsBytes and Opoyi. He has also served as an academic editor for Cactus Communications, where he worked with scholars on manuscripts meant for journal publication.

Shiladitya is based out of Delhi, is an avid reader, and has a keen interest in world affairs, science, philosophy, music, and football.

headline: Iran-US-Israel War LIVE Updates: US strike hits Iran airport, aid flight to India at affected; Indian worker killed in Kuwait drone attack
content:
US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth on Tuesday said American forces will step up military action against Iran if its leadership does not agree to a deal with President Donald Trump, even as he claimed that “regime change has occurred.”
Speaking at a briefing, Hegseth said Iran’s new leadership should be “wiser than the last,” warning that failure to negotiate could trigger operations with “even more intensity.”
He reiterated that US troops remain highly motivated, with a clear focus on completing the mission.
Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, General Dan Caine, provided an operational update, stating that the US Navy has successfully taken out over 150 ships so far.
He also confirmed that B-52 bomber missions have begun, signalling an escalation in long-range strike capabilities.
The developments indicate a further intensification of the conflict, with Washington combining military pressure and diplomatic signalling to push for a resolution.
US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth said troops involved in the ongoing military operation are displaying strong motivation, clarity of mission and an “urgency” to complete objectives, marking a shift from past conflicts.
Speaking at a briefing after visiting deployed forces over the weekend, Hegseth said the trip was an “honour” and that he witnessed “the best of America.” He did not disclose the location due to security reasons.
Highlighting troop sentiment, he recalled an interaction with a female service member who, when asked what she needed, replied: “more bombs.”
Hegseth said the mood among troops contrasts with earlier wars in Iraq and Afghanistan, where uncertainty over timelines and outcomes weighed heavily.
“In this operation, there is clear mission focus and urgency to finish the job,” he said, suggesting a more defined objective and timeline compared to previous prolonged engagements.
US President Donald Trump on Tuesday said countries facing fuel shortages due to disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz should “go to the Strait, and just take it,” signalling a tougher stance on global security commitments.
In a post on Truth Social, Trump said nations such as the United Kingdom must “start learning how to fight for yourself,” adding that the US would no longer step in to help countries that did not support its actions against Iran.
The remarks come as tensions in West Asia continue to disrupt global energy supplies, pushing up oil prices and freight costs.
Trump also claimed that Iran had been “essentially decimated” and suggested the conflict had entered a less intense phase.
The comments are likely to raise concerns among allies, as they indicate a shift towards a more transactional US approach to protecting critical global trade routes.
The European Union has called for an investigation after attacks on UN peacekeepers in Lebanon killed three Indonesian troops during fighting between Israel and Hezbollah. The EU described the attacks as a grave violation of international law and demanded that they stop immediately.
An airstrike hit Beirut’s southern suburbs after the Israeli military warned residents to evacuate the area, according to AFPTV footage. The strike came as Israel’s defence minister said troops would occupy parts of southern Lebanon even after the war ends.
Iran’s foreign minister said Tehran’s attacks in Gulf Arab states are aimed only at US forces, despite reports of civilian sites being hit. His comments to Saudi Arabia come amid rising Gulf anger and pressure on the United States to continue military action in the region.
Italy and France have expressed deep concern over worsening security in Lebanon following recent attacks on UN peacekeepers. The defense ministers stressed the importance of the UNIFIL mission and said Lebanon’s stability is crucial for security across the Mediterranean region.
Myanmar is facing worsening fuel shortages as rationing rules, QR code systems and vehicle restrictions cause long queues at gas stations in major cities. Authorities have taken action against several fuel stations for violations as distribution slows and congestion worsens.
Three Chinese vessels have transited the Strait of Hormuz after coordinating with unspecified parties, according to China’s Foreign Ministry. Beijing also called for an immediate ceasefire, stressing the strategic waterway’s importance for global energy supplies and trade.
An Israeli soldier has been dismissed after making inappropriate comments and allegedly assaulting a CNN crew in the West Bank, including placing a producer in a chokehold. Other soldiers were reprimanded, and the battalion has been suspended pending investigation.
Israel’s military spokesperson says 10 soldiers have died fighting in Lebanon since the start of the Israeli invasion, including four deaths announced Tuesday. As of Friday, the military said 261 troops had been injured, 22 seriously, in fighting since the start of the latest war.
Meanwhile, the UN has condemned Iran’s executions of political prisoners, warning they are being used to suppress dissent during wartime.
Rajiv Memani, President, CII:
Engaging With Govt On Urgent Measures To Ease Ind Pain, Focus On Gas Allocation & Sector Relief
Seeking Urgent Support For MSME Exporters, Particularly On Credit Access & Financing Challenges
Logistics Bottlenecks & Feedstock Availability Are Key Concerns Needing Short-term Intervention
Long-term Concerns Include Currency Volatility And A Widening Current Account Deficit
Rising Energy Imports Straining The Current Account And Require Structural Solutions
Inflation Is Set To Remain Elevated Over The Next Six Months
GDP Growth Will Definitely Take A Hit, Even If The Shock Is Short-lived
Macro Impact Is Already Being Felt Across Key Indicators
Akshay Gulati, Co-Founder, Ship Rocket:
Domestically, fuel prices are well-calibrated—impact is limited so far.
But exporters, especially SMBs, are facing a double whammy of rising costs and disruptions.
Air freight rates have surged 30–70%, making exports unviable.
Shipment costs are so volatile, they can jump 40% within days.
High freight costs are forcing exports to slow and inventory to pile up.
Many shipments are shifting from air to sea, where feasible.
LPG shortages are hitting sectors like textiles, with some plants shutting down.
For many SMBs, this is turning into an existential crisis if it continues.
Domestic logistics remain stable, with just 1–2% cost increase and minor delays.
Sunil Munjal, Past President Confederation of Indian Industry and Chairman Hero Enterprise:
The currency is hovering near 95 and could touch 100 if pressure continues.
Yes, depreciation is a concern, but this could make India more attractive for investors.
A weaker rupee means foreign investors get more value for their money in India.
We should actively pitch India’s strong consumption and population story to global investors.
The downside is real—we have limited tools right now to arrest currency depreciation.
RBI has capped bank positions at $100 million, forcing unwinding of large exposures.
That unwinding could add further pressure in the near term.
R Mukundan, President Designate, CII and Managing Director & CEO, Tata Chemicals Limited:
The shift from LPG to PNG is helping ease supply pressures.
About 60% of demand has been addressed, the rest is being prioritised.
Supply is being diverted to the most critical sectors.
The focus now is on boosting supply, accelerating conversion, and managing demand.
Industry has drawn up an 8-point action plan to stabilise the situation.
This disruption will feed into inflation and impact jobs across sectors.
The immediate supply shock may last 8–9 weeks.
But the economic impact could linger for up to two years.
Industry is committing to maintain price stability during this period.
We are also committed to protecting jobs despite the stress.
Sunil Kant Munjal, Past President Confederation of Indian Industry and Chairman Hero Enterprise:
India must expand crude reserves beyond 9.5 days and diversify sourcing globally.
We need to explore new energy routes—from Africa to Brazil—and secure long-term supply lines.
Rising coal and aviation fuel prices are sharply increasing operating costs.
Industry must now focus inward on efficiency to manage this cost shock.
Government-industry collaboration will be key to driving innovation and long-term resilience.
We need tax incentives, R&D support, and startup push—not just direct subsidies.
R Mukundan, President Designate, CII and Managing Director & CEO Tata Chemicals Limited:
We’re in talks with the government to rework gas allocation for stressed sectors.
The maximum disruption was in LPG, but the situation is improving fast.
Industry allocation has jumped from 20% to nearly 80% in just 10 days.
Most supply disruptions are now behind us, barring a few pockets.
We’re working with the government to resolve last-mile shortages.
Israeli security officials inspected light damage near the Tel Aviv Marina, a popular sailing hub, following a round of incoming strikes while the war in Iran continued.
Overnight, the Israeli military carried out a series of airstrikes targeting roads in the western Bekaa region of eastern Lebanon, according to Al Jazeera.
The strikes appear aimed at isolating towns and villages where Hezbollah maintains a strong presence, including routes linking the west Bekaa to southern Lebanon.
The Bekaa Valley has long served as a key Hezbollah stronghold, and the latest strikes suggest a broader strategy to disrupt supply lines and prevent the movement of fighters, weapons and reinforcements to the south.
At least one Palestinian was killed and several others injured after an Israeli strike hit a group of civilians in Jabalia in northern Gaza, according to the Wafa news agency.
The attack comes as the overall toll from the conflict in Gaza has risen to 72,280 deaths and 172,014 injured since October 7, 2023, the report said.
The US Department of War has announced that it will be holding an “Operation Epic Fury Press Conference” with Secretary of War Pete Hegseth and Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Dan Caine at 8 am EST (6.30 pm IST) today.
ANNOUNCEMENT: pic.twitter.com/XPaloxR9nw
— Department of War ???????? (@DeptofWar) March 31, 2026
Iranian security forces have arrested two individuals in the country’s northwest on charges of sharing sensitive information with US and Israeli intelligence agencies, according to Al Jazeera.
Citing Iran’s Tasnim news agency, the report said the suspects allegedly received cryptocurrency in exchange for providing details about secure locations.
The two were detained in the Osku area of East Azerbaijan province and have been handed over to judicial authorities, the report added.
More than 17 million people in Southern and Eastern Africa could face acute hunger if the Middle East conflict continues to destabilise global markets, a UN analysis says. The report highlights worsening food insecurity, malnutrition and displacement across the region, driven by conflict, climate shocks and funding cuts, with humanitarian agencies cautioning that millions of lives are at risk without urgent support.
Trump and his advisers reportedly concluded that a full‑scale operation to reopen the waterway would extend the war beyond their targeted four‑to‑six‑week timeline.

headline: Does Trump Want Arab Countries to Pay for the War on Iran?
content:
When White House spokeswoman Karoline Leavitt faced questions Monday about whether Arab states should contribute financially to the war effort, much like they did during the 1990 Gulf War, she did not shut the idea down.
"I think it's something the president would be quite interested in calling them to do," she told reporters, adding that it was an idea Donald Trump held and that the public would "hear more from him on."
It is not the first time this kind of thinking, which suggests others should pay for wars waged by America, has surfaced. Sean Hannity, the right-wing commentator with known ties to Trump, said recently that any ceasefire agreement should require Iran itself to repay America, in oil, for the full cost of military operations. "They must agree to repay America in oil for the entire cost of this entire military operation," Hannity said.
Iran, for its part, has flipped that argument around entirely, demanding US compensation for war damage as one of its own conditions at the negotiating table.
The Gulf War Precedent
The comparison to 1990 is not arbitrary. When the US assembled a global coalition to push Iraq out of Kuwait, the financial burden was widely shared. Regional states, along with allies like Germany and Japan, collectively raised $54 billion, worth roughly $134 billion in today's money, to cover Washington's costs.Drone strikes a warehouse in Sharjah, UAE.
The situation now is fundamentally different, though. The US and Israel launched military action against Iran without building that kind of coalition first. No regional buy-in, no allied framework, no shared burden from the outset. Asking countries to pay after the fact, for a war they were not consulted on, is a harder sell.
What Has the War Cost So Far?
The numbers emerging from Washington are significant. Congressional members were briefed in a classified hearing that the first six days of fighting alone ran to $11.3 billion. The Center for Strategic and International Studies put the figure at $16.5 billion by day twelve. With the conflict now in its 31st day, the real total is almost certainly well beyond those estimates.To keep the campaign going and restock Pentagon munitions, the White House is pushing Congress for at least $200 billion in additional military funding.
Pumping Up Prices at the Pump
The war's financial ripple effects are being felt far beyond Washington's budget battles. Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world's most critical oil shipping lanes, has sent energy markets into a spin. According to the American Automobile Association, the average price of a gallon of petrol in the US has crossed $3.99, a jump of more than a dollar compared to before the conflict began.US President Donald Trump
Leavitt pushed back on the idea that this should alarm Americans. The administration's position, she said Monday, is that short-term price pain is the trade-off for a long-term gain, specifically, neutralizing what Washington describes as an enduring Iranian threat to US forces and regional allies.

headline: Did Pete Hegseth Seek to Profit From Iran War? Morgan Stanley-BlackRock ETF Controversy Explained
content:
Fresh scrutiny has emerged around US Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth after a new report from Financial Times claimed his broker explored a major investment in defense-related assets shortly before US-Israeli military action against Iran. According to the report, Hegseth’s broker at Morgan Stanley contacted BlackRock in February regarding a potential multimillion-dollar investment into the firm’s Defense Industrials Active ETF.
The outreach reportedly came just weeks before the US launched military strikes on Tehran, raising questions about timing and intent. However, the proposed investment did not go through. The fund, which launched in May last year, was not yet available for Morgan Stanley clients to purchase at the time of the inquiry.
The report does not clarify how much authority the broker had to act independently or whether Hegseth himself was aware of the attempted investment. Neither BlackRock nor Morgan Stanley has commented publicly.
Department Of Defense Denies Allegations Against Hegseth
The Department of Defense issued a strong denial of allegations of wrongdoing levied against Hegseth. In a post on X, Sean Parnell, assistant to the secretary of defense for public affairs, called FT's reporting "false and fabricated" and stated that neither Hegseth nor any his representatives had approached BlackRock about such investment.
Parnell also demanded that FT retract the story.
"This allegation is entirely false and fabricated. Neither Secretary Hegseth nor any of his representatives approached BlackRock about any such investment. This is yet another baseless, dishonest smear designed to mislead the public. We demand an immediate retraction. Secretary Hegseth and the Department of War remain unwavering in their commitment to the highest standards of ethics and strict adherence to all applicable laws and regulations," Parnell wrote on X.
Timing Raises Questions Amid Broader Market Scrutiny
The episode comes amid growing attention on financial activity preceding major policy decisions by President Donald Trump, particularly those involving geopolitical tensions.
Over the past few months, analysts have highlighted examples where trading activity and wagers in financial and prediction markets seemed to predict significant government actions. These developments have raised alarms among specialists, who are now questioning whether privileged information might have leaked before public disclosures.
While DOD has denied wrongdoing, the timing of the alleged investment, so close to military action involving Iran, has added to the broader debate over transparency and potential conflicts of interest in high-level government circles.
